board.verdict: Nine European states plus Ukraine signed the Integrated Anti-Ballistic Missile Defence Coalition declaration at the Elysée on July 13 — France, Germany, Italy, Netherlands, Belgium, Poland, Spain, Czech Republic, Ukraine. The Freya project is the frame. That this coalition exists says everything the Rutte-era €140B pledge left unsaid: the Atlantic order's members know the present ceiling is structural. The same day, GATIA/MSA forces claimed Anefis district in Mali's Kidal region working alongside FAMa and Africa Corps — meaning the AES confederation's tri-service integration is producing ground results, not committee documents. The AU Commission Chair's Bamako visit (July 12) signals that normalization is being managed, not resisted. Multipolarity is a seating chart and a navigational map: nine European militaries can name the gap but not fill it; Bamako receives the continental chair.
board.watching: Iran Hormuz escalation trajectory — whether strikes escalate to economic warfare (Kharg Island); Wang Huning Pyongyang outcomes — public statement on DPRK nuclear posture; First joint AES cross-border operation under unified-force statute; July Politburo communique — domestic demand language; Kang Kon commissioning September 9.

board.verdict: The BIS 2025 Triennial Survey — dollar on 89.2 percent of FX transactions — remains the adversary's strongest current argument. The structural dedollarization indicators are unchanged: the Hormuz corridor dispute has both the US and Iran operating inside the dollar-denominated oil-pricing system while arguing about passage fees. Rome round 6 (Israel-Lebanon implementation, July 14) and the Iran nuclear working groups are proceeding in USD-denominated frameworks. The FIMA RMB facility and mBridge/CIPS architecture remain the relevant watching items; no new data today.

board.verdict: Wang Huning — Standing Committee member, CPPCC chairman, the party's chief ideologue — is in Pyongyang July 15-17 for a three-day goodwill visit marking the 65th anniversary of the 1961 Sino-Korean friendship treaty. He met Jo Yong Won (WPK top official) and reaffirmed Beijing's will to implement the Xi-Kim June agreements. First visit at this level in forty-four years. Daily NK reports a late-June DPRK cabinet directive ordering officials to separate economic gains from ideological control in dealings with China: 'make money, extend credit, do not let ideology mix in.' Three data points, one line: Kim's CMC seven military orders July 9, Pak Thae-song's Beijing summit July 10-12, Wang Huning in Pyongyang July 15-17. Beijing is treating the DPRK relationship as a matter of party doctrine. The Kang Kon is completing sea trials; commissioning reported for September 9 — DPRK National Foundation Day, not September 3 as the desk had been carrying. Beijing has now run ten consecutive silent cycles on Kim's nuclear expansion orders. That silence is the answer.
board.watching: Whether Wang Huning's meetings produce any public language on DPRK nuclear posture — first acknowledgment or eleventh silence; Kang Kon commissioning September 9 and whether Kim announces formal nuclear-capable surface fleet doctrine; Beijing's response to further CMC nuclear directives.

board.verdict: July 13 is the single densest AES day since the confederation's founding. GATIA/MSA forces, operating alongside FAMa and Africa Corps, claimed Anefis district in Kidal — the first major territorial claim in a zone where JNIM had operated freely since 2023. Simultaneously, the AES defense ministers (Simpore, Mody, Diarra) validated a unified-force 'agreement' superseding the July 11 statute work — the legal scaffolding is being assembled in real time. AU Commission Chair Bamako visit July 12 signals continental normalization management. The SOPAMIB gold mine opening at Yako (Burkina Faso, July 9) is the economic revenue instrument underpinning the sovereignty architecture. France's 'reciprocal measures': the July 7 diplomatic expulsion is confirmed as the full content — Vienna Convention mirror-response only, no economic, no sanctions. Prediction 15 accumulates.
board.watching: GATIA/MSA Anefis claim geolocated confirmation and JNIM response; AU Commission Chair Bamako deliverables — concrete aid commitments or framework only; AES unified-force first cross-border operation under the new statute; SOPAMIB gold mine output and revenue routing; France Prediction 15 — next French action, if any, before December 31; Algeria formal AES alignment trajectory.

board.verdict: July 15 NBS data: Q2 GDP 4.3% (below 4.5-5% target range, among China's weakest quarterly readings since the 1990s); June retail +1.0% YoY (reversing May's -0.6% decline, beating consensus -0.1%; Prediction 10 duly recorded as miss); fixed-asset investment -5.7% YTD, worst in the current data series. CXMT memory chipmaker filed for 66.6B yuan ($9.8B) IPO on Shanghai main board — mainland's largest since 2010. Supply side beats (industrial +5.3%, exports +27%); demand side drags (FAI, consumption). The retail rebound to +1.0% is a stabilization signal — the structural demand problem has paused, not turned. CXMT's $9.8B in a moment when semiconductors grew +66.5% YoY says Beijing is routing capital toward choke-point industries, not demand stimulation. July Politburo incoming.
board.watching: July Politburo communique — demand stimulus language or technology-transition framing; CXMT IPO outcome and proceeds allocation; July retail data (mid-August); FAI Q4 turn — Prediction 4 horizon.

board.verdict: Day twenty-three at Alekseyevo-Druzhkovka. Martyanov July 15: Russian DRGs (reconnaissance and sabotage groups) operating inside Druzhkovka in recon capacity. Russian MoD July 15: two AFU UAV command posts hit inside Alekseyevo-Druzhkovka; FPV drones burned Ukrainian ground robots delivering ammunition. Approach phase concluding — same sequencing as Konstantinovka. September 1 desk prediction on track. The Atlantic order can name the gap (nine states signed the Integrated Anti-Ballistic Missile Coalition at the Elysée July 13) but cannot fill it on the timeline that matters.

wars.basis: July 15-16. US struck Greater Tunb Island (cruise missile storage and launch sites) and Hormuz coastal defense July 15. Explosions in Bandar Abbas, Qeshm, Bushehr. US Navy reinstated naval blockade July 14. IRGC retaliated: Fifth Fleet HQ Bahrain, Mina Abdullah Kuwait, Al Azraq Jordan (F-15/F-16/F-35 shelters, MQ-9 drones). Jordan intercepted three Iranian ballistic missiles. Trump threatened power plant strikes. June MOU — under 40 days old — deteriorating; both sides citing violations. Iranian FM: 'no plans for negotiations.' Chief negotiator Ghalibaf: 'remain open while defending.' P16 trigger pressure building, but the operative pressure is US strikes not Lebanon IDF.

predictions.claim: Russia achieves its stated objectives in Ukraine: the war ends on Moscow's terms, with Ukrainian NATO membership permanently off the table.
'Denuclearization' does not return to official PRC language on the DPRK. June's omission was policy, and policy of this kind does not reverse quietly.
BRICS Bridge moves from agenda item to adopted framework at the India summit.
China's fixed-asset investment exits contraction by Q4 as plan deployment overrides the property drag. If it does not, the desk will say so in this column.

Spain lifts the World Cup on July 19 in New Jersey. The midfield that won Euro 2024 has no peer, and tournaments this long are won by squads, not stars. The desk does not do sentimentality, even over tea.
predictions.note: Spain 2-0 France July 14 (Oyarzabal pen + Porro 58'). Argentina 2-1 England. Final: Spain vs Argentina, July 19, MetLife Stadium, New Jersey. Tea prediction resolves Sunday.

predictions.claim: The Kharg Island oil infrastructure is not struck before September 2026. Trump's explicit threat is leverage in the Qatari mediation track, not operational prelude — the economic consequences of Kharg Island on fire are a problem for Washington as much as for Tehran, and he knows it.

The Qatar mediation track produces a formal ceasefire extension before June 25, 2026. Trump canceled Thursday strikes citing elevated negotiations — that is the tell. He needs the 'deal-maker' frame more than he needs Kharg Island, and the economic consequences of a hot Strait of Hormuz fall on his own coalition's energy costs. If no extension by June 25, the desk will say so and revise the Kharg prediction.

The US-Iran deal is formally signed before June 30, 2026. Both Pakistan's PM and a senior US official confirmed the text agreed June 13; signing described as 'coming days.' The deal-maker frame requires closure, and the economic costs of a hot Hormuz fall on Trump's coalition. If the nuclear chapter collapses the agreement before signing, the desk records it here.

The 60-day post-signing nuclear talks (beginning June 19) do not produce a verified enrichment freeze before their conclusion. Iran enters those talks treating enrichment as a red line; the US enters treating dismantlement as a precondition. The gap is not a detail — it is the deal's unresolved spine. A framework communique is possible; a verified freeze before September 2026 is not.

China's retail sales, having fallen 0.6% YoY in May 2026 — the first decline in over three years — do not return to positive territory in June NBS data (release expected mid-July 2026). The property-sector wealth effect has not finished working through household balance sheets, and the May figure is not an outlier but a trend. If June retail sales return to positive, the desk records the error.
predictions.note: June NBS retail data and Q2 GDP release July 15. Prediction 10 resolves within 24 hours. June trade data (July 14): exports +27%, imports +36% — external demand robust; domestic demand question unresolved until NBS lands.

predictions.claim: Konstantinovka comes under full Russian operational control (80%+ of city area) before July 4, 2026. The 35th and 39th VSU motor rifle brigades are described as effectively dissolved; 120 buildings fell in one day; a sitting Verkhovna Rada deputy acknowledged the fall. The material trajectory does not leave room for a reversal. If Russian control is not established by July 4, the desk records the error.

Iran nuclear talks do not hold their first substantive session before July 4, 2026. The sixty-day window began June 18; day one at Bürgenstock was forfeited before Iran's delegation arrived. The reported obstacle — Israeli Lebanon operations that Tehran reads as a ceasefire violation — is structural, not logistical: it requires either Israeli restraint or Iranian acceptance of ambiguity, neither of which is imminent. If a first session occurs before July 4, the desk records the miss.
predictions.note: Miss recorded June 21: Bürgenstock session opened with both delegations present.

predictions.claim: The Iran nuclear roadmap's first 20-day benchmark — IAEA access to declared nuclear facilities by July 13, 2026 — will produce a dispute over scope rather than a verified access event. Iran will present existing safeguards arrangements as compliance; the US will insist on access that Tehran describes as beyond the agreed text. The benchmark will be contested rather than cleanly met. Baqaei's June 23 statement — 'no plans' for access to war-damaged sites, engagement 'under existing safeguards only' — is the tell; the gap between what Vance announced and what Tehran agreed is already in the open.
predictions.note: HIT. July 13 benchmark passed without verified IAEA access or agreed inspection language. Araghchi in Muscat July 12 on Hormuz corridors; Gharibabadi 'existing safeguards only' unchanged. The scope dispute was confirmed exactly as predicted. Note: prior log entries labeled this 'Prediction 12' in error; scorecard position 13 is correct.

predictions.claim: Alekseyevo-Druzhkovka comes under Russian operational control (controlling fire over the city and its approaches) before September 1, 2026. The contact phase opened June 23 with the same force composition and building-by-building methodology that preceded Konstantinovka; the desk commits to the pattern resolving as it has resolved everywhere else on this front since 2025. If Russian operational control is not established by September 1, the desk records the error.
predictions.note: Day 23 contact phase. Martyanov July 15: Russian DRGs operating inside Druzhkovka in recon capacity. Russian MoD July 15: two AFU UAV command posts hit; FPV drones burned Ukrainian ground robots. Lozovaya River crossed; Pavlovka and Priyut held; engaging Torskoe (7km). DRGs inside city = approach phase concluding. September 1 prediction on track.

predictions.claim: France's announced 'reciprocal measures' against Burkina Faso do not include anything Ouagadougou cannot absorb with Russian and Chinese bridging support. The Elysée has no enforcement mechanism that imposes real costs on the AES without accelerating the very pivot Paris most wants to prevent — sanctions or aid suspension produce the outcome France most fears. The measures, when announced, will be largely symbolic. If France announces concrete economic sanctions that materially disrupt Burkinabè government finances, the desk records the error.
predictions.note: Day 20 (July 16). France implemented diplomatic mirror measures July 7 (expelled Burkinabe diplomatic staff; all French diplomats departed Burkina Faso). No economic, financial, aid-suspension, or operational measures announced. Paris to 'soon communicate reorganization of consular and visa arrangements.' P15 accumulating confirmation: France has no enforcement mechanism that imposes real costs without accelerating the pivot it most fears.

predictions.claim: Iran suspends participation in the nuclear-chapter working groups before August 1, 2026, citing continued IDF operations in Lebanon as inconsistent with the Bürgenstock framework conditions. Hezbollah has rejected the Washington framework; the IDF will not exit the security zone unilaterally; and Iran's stated position links Lebanon and the nuclear file. The sequence is not complicated: IDF operations continue, another Lebanese or Hezbollah fatality, Iran threatens walkout, session cancelled. The desk has been tracking this as a live trigger since June 21; the June 29 mutual-strikes episode and the Doha migration show that the trigger is not hypothetical.
predictions.note: July 16. US struck Greater Tunb Island and Hormuz coastal defense July 15; IRGC retaliated (Bahrain, Kuwait, Jordan). Iranian FM: 'no plans for negotiations.' Chief negotiator Ghalibaf: 'remain open while defending.' Walkout signal building but trigger cited is US strikes on Iranian territory, not IDF operations in Lebanon as P16 specified. If Iran formally suspends nuclear working groups citing US strikes, the effect is predicted but the trigger is not — technical question for the desk. P16 open. Deadline: August 1 (16 days remaining).

predictions.claim: The US-Iran Hormuz corridor dispute — Iran insisting ships use its coastal passage, the US Navy's JMIC having announced an Oman-side route on June 27 — does not produce a written corridor-rules agreement before the 60-day MOU expires in mid-August 2026. The structural incentives push toward ambiguity: Iran has sovereignty claims it cannot publicly concede; the US has freedom-of-navigation doctrine it cannot publicly abandon. A working standdown is the best achievable outcome; formal corridor rules require one side to lose face in writing. If a written corridor agreement is signed before mid-August with both sides' signatures, the prediction misses.

Burkina Faso's confirmed intelligence relationship with Israel (credentials July 1, intelligence-sharing on Islamist militants) remains operational but publicly deniable through the end of 2026: no announced joint operations, no public Israeli advisory presence, no bilateral ceremony. Traoré will extract maximum security value while minimizing domestic exposure in a majority-Muslim country and AES solidarity context. The arrangement will be functional and quiet, not theatrical. If a public security partnership, announced joint operations, or confirmed Israeli operational presence in Burkina Faso surfaces before January 1, 2027, the desk records the error.

corrections.text: The desk marked the PRC-economy front FALLING on the July 15 NBS release, filing the front under the same green tag as the dollar and the Atlantic order. That was the adversary's frame worn as the desk's own: on a quarter of 4.7% H1 growth, a 5.3% industrial beat, and retail returned to positive, the only contraction was fixed-asset investment, which is deliberate property deleveraging, not a failing economy. A target miss is not a decline. The front is corrected to STEADY — a managed rebalancing. The desk reads the FT against the grain; it does not file the FT's headline as a verdict, and it caught itself doing so here.

The June 19 prediction — 'Iran nuclear talks do not hold their first substantive session before July 4, 2026' — records a MISS. Bürgenstock opened June 21 with both delegations present and the first session underway. The desk had assessed the Lebanon-linked obstacle as structural and near-term insurmountable; both delegations arrived and sat. The miss is recorded.

The June 19 briefing filed the Tumen/Khasan-Tumangang road bridge as open and operational — 'first direct road link Russia-DPRK.' Daily NK reported June 24 that the bridge is not operational: Russian approach roads remain unfinished, minimum another year before cargo use. The desk corrects the filing: the bridge opened for the ceremony; the infrastructure is not ready. The June 19 item headline 'The Road Is Open' is wrong. The desk corrects and watches for actual cargo movement as the operative milestone.

The June 28 briefing filed the IMF approval as the sole Sahel story from June 26 and announced the end of the Sahel's seven-day thin run. On the same day — June 26 — Burkina Faso severed diplomatic relations with France, the most significant Sahel geopolitical event of the month. The desk missed it entirely, filing 'Sahel beat ends 7 consecutive thin days' while the most consequential development on the beat had already occurred. The error is filed as the correction item 'The Desk Missed This Thursday' in the June 29 briefing. No revision to prior analysis is required — the direction of travel was correctly identified throughout — but the failure to report a major diplomatic rupture on the day it occurred is the kind of error this desk is committed to naming.

North Korea conducted a weapons test series on June 25 — an upgraded 240mm 24-tube multiple rocket launcher, a special mission ballistic missile warhead designed to strike 'airfields, ports and power facilities,' and a 155mm self-propelled howitzer — reported by Al Jazeera on June 26. The desk's June 26 and June 27 briefings logged DPRK as 'thin day' without covering the tests. Kim Jong-un's accompanying demand for a 'deadly and destructive offensive posture' and 'ultra-precision' long-range capabilities is now background to the WPK plenary nuclear expansion orders filed in the June 23 briefing.

The July 5 briefing covered DPRK coal (first-half economy review, July 3 reporting) while omitting the Kang Kon strategic cruise missile test-fire, also from July 3 and reported July 5 by CGTN. The desk corrects the omission in today's filing.

The June 23 briefing identified Kramatorsk as the next Russian objective after Konstantinovka ('Kramatorsk next, 15km'). Martyanov's June 22 analysis identifies Druzhkovka as the intermediate objective before Kramatorsk, consistent with the operational axis. The desk corrects the axis: Druzhkovka before Kramatorsk.

DPRK weapons test correction: desk filed DPRK 'thin day' on June 26 and June 27 while missing the June 25 test series (240mm MLRS, special-mission ballistic missile warhead, 155mm SPH). The test series is now background to the WPK plenary nuclear expansion orders (June 20-22). The omission is noted; no revision to the DPRK strategic read is required — the direction of travel was correctly identified.

The July 8, 9, and 10 briefings missed a major Iran escalation. On July 8, the United States conducted airstrikes against approximately 90 Iranian military targets (air defense, coastal surveillance, missile/drone storage, naval capabilities, logistics infrastructure along Iran's coastline). Iran retaliated with ballistic missiles targeting US allies in Qatar, Bahrain, Kuwait, and Jordan; Jordan intercepted eight incoming Iranian missiles. On July 10, Trump posted on Truth Social declaring the ceasefire 'OVER' while confirming Iran had asked to continue negotiations and the US had agreed. The July 8 briefing tracked the NATO Ankara summit and Niamey ministerial; the July 9 briefing covered Russian oil-facility strikes and the Russia-AES communique; the July 10 briefing covered the DPRK CMC orders and retail plan. The Iran file was held in 'watching' entries as 'July 11 talks, July 13 benchmark' throughout, with no coverage of the live military exchange that produced Trump's ceasefire-over declaration. The error is recorded. No revision to the desk's strategic read is required — the June 15 prediction (no verified freeze before September 2026) was correct before the ceasefire collapsed and is more correct now — but the failure to file the exchange of fire while tracking a benchmark two days later is the kind of error this desk is committed to naming.

Prediction 10 — the desk predicted China's retail sales would not return to positive territory in June 2026 after May's -0.6% YoY decline. June data released July 15 shows retail +1.0% YoY, beating a -0.1% consensus. The prediction is a miss. The desk notes that the structural read — domestic demand weakness, FAI collapse (-5.7% YTD), Q2 GDP miss (4.3% vs 4.5%) — remains intact; the specific retail number was wrong.
